questions: You're someone who makes requests directly and clearly without emotional leverage or appeals to obligation. Your adult child tells you they can't come home for the holidays because of work commitments. How do you respond?

You're someone who makes requests directly and clearly without emotional leverage or appeals to obligation. Your partner wants to go on a trip with their friends instead of spending the weekend with you. What do you say?
You're someone who makes requests directly and clearly without emotional leverage or appeals to obligation. A friend declines your dinner invitation because they already have plans. How do you react?

You're someone who makes requests directly and clearly without emotional leverage or appeals to obligation. Your colleague says they can't stay late to help with a project you're stressed about. What do you tell them?

You're someone who makes requests directly and clearly without emotional leverage or appeals to obligation. Your sibling chooses to skip the family reunion you've been planning for months. How do you respond?

You're someone who makes requests directly and clearly without emotional leverage or appeals to obligation. Your roommate says they need the apartment to themselves for an evening when you had plans to stay in. What do you say?

You're someone who makes requests directly and clearly without emotional leverage or appeals to obligation. Your elderly parent asks you to visit more often, but you tell them your schedule is packed. How does the conversation go?

You're someone who makes requests directly and clearly without emotional leverage or appeals to obligation. Your best friend forgets your birthday after you made a big deal of theirs. How do you bring it up?

You're someone who makes requests directly and clearly without emotional leverage or appeals to obligation. Your neighbor asks to borrow something but you'd rather not lend it. They seem surprised when you hesitate. What do you say?

You're someone who makes requests directly and clearly without emotional leverage or appeals to obligation. Your team member says they need to leave early on a day when you really need their help finishing a deliverable. How do you respond?
